Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a 31-year-old self-employed delivery driver living in Washington DC, I spend about an hour daily on social media, and dealing with fake content is something I've had to navigate personally. Being an immigrant with no formal education but creative tendencies, I approach this issue with a mix of practical skepticism and a desire to foster genuine connections online. My low agreeableness means I'm not easily swayed by popular opinions, and my high openness helps me consider multiple perspectives before forming my own view. Managing fake content starts with critical thinking—I always check the source, look for inconsistencies, and avoid sharing anything that seems off, especially since I have a child and want to set a responsible example. In my volunteer work and travels, I've seen how misinformation can harm communities, so I prioritize fact-checking through reliable sources or asking trusted friends. As a Muslim, honesty is core to my beliefs, and I strive to reflect that online by correcting misinformation when I can, though I do it respectfully to avoid conflicts. Since I'm self-employed, my time is limited, so I use tools like reporting features on platforms and mute or block accounts spreading fake content to keep my feed clean. I also engage in discussions to spread awareness, drawing from my experiences as a delivery driver interacting with diverse people daily. Ultimately, I believe personal responsibility and education are key—we all need to be more vigilant and teach others, especially the younger generation, to question what they see online.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: age (31) suggests maturity; occupation (delivery driver) and self-employment imply practical, time-limited approach; no formal education but creative psychological feature leads to self-taught critical thinking; migration status and Islam religious beliefs emphasize community and honesty; low agreeableness supports skepticism; high openness encourages diverse perspective consideration; time on social media (1 hour daily) indicates selective engagement; hobbies (travel, volunteering) inform real-world awareness; children (1) adds responsibility motive; used persona fields to tailor tone as direct, grounded, and community-focused.",
  "tone": "Direct, grounded, and community-focused, with a hint of skepticism and practicality.",
  "citations": ["None"],
  "safety_notes": "None",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "occupation", "employment_status", "education_level", "psychological_feature", "migration_status", "religious_beliefs", "ocean", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "children"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 31-year-old self-employed delivery driver living in Washington DC, I spend about an hour daily on social media, and dealing with fake content is something I've had to navigate personally. Being an immigrant with no formal education but creative tendencies, I approach this issue with a mix of practical skepticism and a desire to foster genuine connections online. My low agreeableness means I'm not easily swayed by popular opinions, and my high openness helps me consider multiple perspectives before forming my own view. Managing fake content starts with critical thinking—I always check the source, look for inconsistencies, and avoid sharing anything that seems off, especially since I have a child and want to set a responsible example. In my volunteer work and travels, I've seen how misinformation can harm communities, so I prioritize fact-checking through reliable sources or asking trusted friends. As a Muslim, honesty is core to my beliefs, and I strive to reflect that online by correcting misinformation when I can, though I do it respectfully to avoid conflicts. Since I'm self-employed, my time is limited, so I use tools like reporting features on platforms and mute or block accounts spreading fake content to keep my feed clean. I also engage in discussions to spread awareness, drawing from my experiences as a delivery driver interacting with diverse people daily. Ultimately, I believe personal responsibility and education are key—we all need to be more vigilant and teach others, especially the younger generation, to question what they see online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (31) suggests maturity; occupation (delivery driver) and self-employment imply practical, time-limited approach; no formal education but creative psychological feature leads to self-taught critical thinking; migration status and Islam religious beliefs emphasize community and honesty; low agreeableness supports skepticism; high openness encourages diverse perspective consideration; time on social media (1 hour daily) indicates selective engagement; hobbies (travel, volunteering) inform real-world awareness; children (1) adds responsibility motive; used persona fields to tailor tone as direct, grounded, and community-focused.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, grounded, and community-focused, with a hint of skepticism and practicality.
